questions.question: Define an array and explain its key characteristics.
keyPoints: Array is a collection of elements of the same data type
Elements are stored in contiguous memory locations
Direct access using index (starts from 0)
Fixed size in static arrays, variable in dynamic arrays
Homogeneous elements - same data type

questions.question: Explain the difference between row-major and column-major order for 2D arrays.
keyPoints: Row-major: elements stored row by row (first row complete, then second)
Column-major: elements stored column by column
Used in different languages: C/C++/Java use row-major, Fortran/MATLAB use column-major
Affects calculation of element addresses in memory

questions.question: What is a sparse matrix? Explain its array-based representation.
keyPoints: Sparse matrix has most elements as zero
Memory inefficient to store all elements including zeros
Represented using compact forms: CSR, CSC, or triplet form
Triplet form stores: row index, column index, value of non-zero elements
Reduces storage from O(m×n) to O(number of non-zeros)

questions.question: Explain dynamic memory allocation for arrays in C using malloc, calloc, and realloc.
keyPoints: malloc: allocates specified bytes, returns void pointer, memory contains garbage values
calloc: allocates and initializes all bytes to zero
realloc: resizes previously allocated memory block
Memory allocated from heap (not stack) for dynamic arrays
Must use free() to deallocate memory to prevent leaks

questions.question: Derive the formula for calculating address of an element in a one-dimensional array.
keyPoints: Base Address (B) is address of first element
Each element occupies 'size' bytes
For element at index i: Address = B + (i × size)
Index starts from 0 in most languages
Example: int arr[10], base=1000, size=4, addr of arr[3]=1000+(3×4)=1012

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of arrays over linked lists?
keyPoints: Advantages: O(1) random access, better cache locality, simple implementation
Disadvantages: fixed size (static), expensive insertions/deletions (require shifting)
Linked lists: dynamic size, O(1) insert/delete at any position, but no random access
Choice depends on use case requirements

questions.question: Explain how polynomials can be represented using arrays with an example.
keyPoints: Polynomial coefficients stored in array indices representing powers
Array index = power of x, value at index = coefficient
Example: P(x) = 5x³ + 2x + 1 stored as arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=0, arr[3]=5
Degree = highest index with non-zero coefficient
Efficient for dense polynomials, less efficient for sparse ones

questions.question: Write pseudocode and C code for inserting an element into a sorted array at the correct position. Analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Find position by searching, shift elements right, insert element
Time complexity: O(n) for shifting in worst case (insert at beginning)
Space complexity: O(1) as in-place operation
Must handle overflow if array is full
Maintain sorted order after insertion

questions.question: Explain the concept of dynamic arrays. How does ArrayList in Java or vector in C++ handle resizing? What is amortized time complexity?
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays grow/shrink at runtime
When capacity reached, allocate new array (typically 2x size)
Copy all elements to new array - takes O(n) time
Amortized O(1) for append operation because occasional resize averages out
Load factor threshold (e.g., 0.75) triggers resizing
Advantage: combines array benefits with flexibility

questions.question: Write pseudocode and C code for deleting an element from an array. Explain handling of all cases (beginning, middle, end).
keyPoints: Algorithm: Find element to delete, shift elements left to fill gap
Case 1 (beginning): shift all n-1 elements - O(n)
Case 2 (middle): shift elements after position - O(n)
Case 3 (end): no shifting required - O(1)
Worst case time complexity: O(n)
Space complexity: O(1)
Handle underflow if array is empty

questions.question: Derive the formula for calculating address of an element in a 2D array stored in row-major order. Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: For array A[m][n] with base address B and element size S
Address of A[i][j] = B + ((i × n) + j) × S
Where i = row index, j = column index, n = number of columns
Example: 3×4 array, base=1000, size=4, find A[2][2]
Index must be validated before accessing
